coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and electrochemically-modulated optical properties of viologen and carbon dots hybrid glass composite films
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yan
Ma, Qiang
Berda, Erik B.
Liu, Xincai
Wang, Ce
Chao, Danming
coredata.dc:description: The electrochemical manipulation of optical properties will be essential in engineering next generation intelligent displays, wearable devices, and smart sensors. The rational design of electrochemically-modulated optical materials, especially those with multiple regulatory capacities, remains an unmet research challenge. We present here an optical composite, featuring multiple electrochemically-modulated properties, prepared through an electrochemistry-assisted hydrolytic crosslinking reaction between electroactive viologen and fluorescent carbon dots. Upon the electrochemical manipulation of the redox states of the viologen moieties, the resultant composite film exhibits interesting multistage regulated chromism with great color contrast, moderate switching rates, good coloration efficiency, and enhanced cycling stability. We demonstrate a unique sequential multistage regulated electrofluorochromism resulting from the inner filter effect and synergistic interplay between the electroactive viologen units and the fluorescent carbon dots. Furthermore, this optical composite reveals electrogenerated chemiluminescence with acceptable electrochemiluminescence intensity, attributed to the intrinsic behavior of carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Viologen
Electrochromic
Electrofluorochromic
Electrochemically-modulated

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of viologen and carbon dots hybrid glass composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation of viologen and carbon dots hybrid glass composite films.
UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra (a), FTIR spectra (b) and TEM images (c) of CDs and CDs-Si. The excitation wavelength is 390 nm.

(a) CV of hydrolysable mixture (CDs-Si and Viologen-Si) coated on ITO substrate with the potentials range of −1.2 V–0 V in 0.1 M KCl solution. The scan rate was 100 mV/s (b) EIS of hydrolysable mixture/ITO electrode with the increase of CV switching. (c) SEM images of VCG composite film after 20 CV cycles, and the inset shows the SEM image of hydrolysable mixture coated on ITO substrate before the crosslinking reaction.

(a) Transmittance spectra of VCG/ITO electrode in 1.0 M KCl solution at different potentials (−1.2 V–0 V) with a residence time of 300 s; inset shows photographs of VCG/ITO electrode at different potentials. (b) Molecular structures of viologen segments at various oxidation states. (c) Transmittance changes of VCG/ITO electrode monitored at 400 nm (first 150 cycles) with various potential ranges of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V. The residence time was fixed at 15 s. (d) Switching times of VCG/ITO electrode at 400 nm with different potential range. (e) Transmittance changes of VCG/ITO electrode monitored at 550 nm (first 150 cycles) with various potential ranges of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V. The residence time was fixed at 15 s. (f) Switching times of VCG/ITO electrode at 550 nm with different potential ranges.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of VCG/ITO electrode at different potentials (0~-1.2 V) with a residence time of 300 s. (b) Fluorescence change of VCG/ITO electrode monitored at 600 nm (first 40 cycles) under different repeated potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V with a residence time of 30 s. (c) The switching times of VCG/ITO electrode under the repeated potentials steppings. The excitation wavelength is 410 nm. (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay profiles of hydrolytic crosslinked CDs-Si and VCG on ITO substrates under excitation at 378 nm.
(a) ECL-potential response of VCG composite at a scan rate of 100 mV/s (b) ECL curves of VCG composite at a continuous scan mode.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and electrochemically-modulated optical properties of viologen and carbon dots hybrid glass composite films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Materials Science Program, University of New Hampshire, Durham, NH, 03824, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Materials Science Program

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Materials Science Program, University of New Hampshire, Durham, New Hampshire, 03824.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrochemical manipulation of optical properties will be essential in engineering next generation intelligent displays, wearable devices, and smart sensors. The rational design of electrochemically-modulated optical materials, especially those with multiple regulatory capacities, remains an unmet research challenge. We present here an optical composite, featuring multiple electrochemically-modulated properties, prepared through an electrochemistry-assisted hydrolytic crosslinking reaction between electroactive viologen and fluorescent carbon dots. Upon the electrochemical manipulation of the redox states of the viologen moieties, the resultant composite film exhibits interesting multistage regulated chromism with great color contrast, moderate switching rates, good coloration efficiency, and enhanced cycling stability. We demonstrate a unique sequential multistage regulated electrofluorochromism resulting from the inner filter effect and synergistic interplay between the electroactive viologen units and the fluorescent carbon dots. Furthermore, this optical composite reveals electrogenerated chemiluminescence with acceptable electrochemiluminescence intensity, attributed to the intrinsic behavior of carbon dots.

A novel optical composite composed of electroactive viologen and fluorescent carbon dots was fabricated through electrochemistry-assisted hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. Due to the inherent functionality and interplay of viologen and carbon dots, the resultant composite film displays multiple electrochemical-modulated optical character including electrochromism, electrofluorochromism, and electrochemiluminescence.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Optical materials continue to garner research attention in recent years due to potential applications as sensors, bioimaging, memory media and displays [1–6]. It remains an urgent need and critical challenge to rationally adjust and control optical properties of such materials for advanced intelligent applications [7–10]. Hitherto most efforts focus on electrochemically modulated optical materials whose optical properties can change according to the input of an electrochemical signal [11–17]. Compared with other regulatory methods (light, temperature, pH, ionic strength, etc.), electrochemical modulation has several incomparable advantages, such as rapid response, reversibility, and high efficiency. Recent years have seen considerable effort toward the electrochemical manipulation of optical properties including electrochromism (redox-switchable color), electrofluorochromism (electrochemical switching of fluorescence), and electrochemiluminescence (electrochemical light generation), etc. Generally, most of these materials exhibit only one specific optical property modulated by electrochemical methods. Some electrochromic and electrofluorochromic dual-switching materials have recently been designed and prepared through the introduction of fluorophores into the molecular architecture of electrochromic materials [18–22]. The interplay and/or energy transfer between electroactive moiety and fluorescence emission moiety would play a crucial role in the electrochemical manipulation of fluorescence property. To further improve their promising application, it appears interesting to rationally design optical materials featuring multiple electrochemically-modulated properties.

Viologen, more formally known as 1,1′-disubstituted 4,4′-bipyridinium ions, is a class of electroactive molecule with three different redox states, i.e. a dication (V2+), a radical cation (V·+), and a di-reduced neutral compound (V0) [ [23–25]]. Its unique redox transition, good electrochemical reversibility and structural tunability make viologen an optimal unit to construct electroactive materials. Likewise, carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a photoluminescent carbonaceous optical material featuring low toxicity, tunable fluorescence, and photostability [26–28]. Generally, the surface of CDs is decorated with oxygen-containing groups such as amino, epoxy, ether, carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups, depending on their synthetic strategies [29–31]. This tunable surface functionalization will offer CDs enormous potential in preparation of optical materials.

Here, we demonstrate a novel optical composite composed of electroactive viologens and fluorescent CDs through an electrochemistry-assisted hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. Due to their inherent functionality and the interplay of viologen and CDs, the resultant composite film displays multiple electrochemically modulated optical characteristics including electrochromism, electrofluorochromism, and electrochemiluminescence. This work offers a versatile and scalable strategy to prepare multiple electrochemically-modulated optical devices aimed at advanced multipurpose intelligent applications.

3-(Triethoxysilyl)propyl isocyanate, 4,4′-bipyridine, 3-bromopropylamine hydrobromide (BPAHB), ammonium hexafluorophosphate and citric acid were purchased from Aladdin. Acetonitrile, dichloromethane, acetone, ethanol, dimethyl formamide (DMF) and ammonium hydroxide were obtained from commercial sources and used as received without further purification. Optically transparent Indium–Tin Oxide (ITO) glass substrates were purchased from Xiang Science & Technology Co. Ltd (Shenzhen) and used as working electrode substrates.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on a BRUKER VECTOR 22 Spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra in deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide were acquired using a VARIAN-300 spectrometer at room temperature. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on a CHI 660E Electrochemical Workstation (CH Instruments, USA) with a conventional three-electrode cell, which was assembled in 1.0 M KCl solution, using Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode, and working electrode, respectively. UV–vis spectra were collected using UV-3101 PC Spectrometer (SHIMADZU). Fluorescence data were collected on F97Pro fluorospectro photometer (Lengguang, Shanghai). The fluorescent lifetimes of hydrolytic crosslinked CDs-Si and VCG samples were measured on an Edinburgh FLS-980 with an EPL-375 optical laser. The electrochemiluminescence detection system consisted of a BPCL ultraweak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China). Integrated emission detection was carried out in the wavelength region of 450–650 nm. The conventional three-electrode cell was carried out in 0.1 M potassium persulfate 10 mM phosphate buffered solution. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova NanoSEM 450) was used to characterize the morphologies of the composite film before and after the CV switching. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEM-2200FS) was used to characterize the morphology of the CDs before and after the modification. The hydrophilicity of the films was measured using a DSA-30 drop-shape analysis system (Kruess) at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of viologen and carbon dots hybrid glass composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
1,1′-Bis(3-aminopropyl)-4,4′-bipyridinium bis(hexafluorophosphate) was prepared according to the previous literature [32]. The synthetic route is depicted in Fig. 1 . 4,4′-Dipyridyl (3.12 g, 20 mmol) and BPAHB (17.52 g, 80 mmol) was mixed in 150 mL CH3CN with magnetic stirring. Then it was heated and refluxed for 72 h. The resultant mixture was cooled down to the room temperature and filtered. The obtained precipitate was then transferred to the water. Excess NH4PF6 was added into the above solution to exchange the counter anions, resulting in crude 1,1′-bis(3-aminopropyl)-4,4′-bipyridinium bis(hexafluorophosphate). After filtration, the crude product was washed with sodium hydroxide solution, distilled water, and THF, respectively. Finally the product was dried under vacuum at 40 °C for 12 h. Yield: 71%. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, ppm): δ = 9.30 (t, 4H, Ar–H next to N+), δ = 8.76 (t, 4H, Ar–H), δ = 5.98 (s, 4H, –NH2), δ = 4.86 (t, 4H, –CH2-next to pyridine ring), δ = 2.89 (t, 4H, –CH2-next to –NH2), δ = 2.30 (m, 4H, –CH2-). FTIR (KBr, cm−1): 3342 (m, υN-H), 3080 (w, υC-H of pyridine ring), 2972 (s, υC-H), 1651 (m, υC
N + of pyridine ring), 1568 (m, υC
C of pyridinering), 1450 (m, δC-H), 1297 (s, υC-N), 1020 (m, δC-H), 842 (m, δC-H), 755(m, δC-H), 560 (m, δC-H).

Amino-decorated carbon dots were prepared by carbonization of citric acid with ammonia according to the literature [33]. CDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying, and then transferred to DMF. The resultant CDs aqueous dispersion and CDs DMF dispersion were stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator for further characterization and use. To decorate the surface of CDs, 3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl isocyanate was added into CDs DMF dispersion under magnetic stirring with the mass ratio of 0.086. The resultant hydrolysable siloxane-decorated CDs were defined as CDs-Si. The obtained CDs and CDs-Si were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, and TEM.

CDs (0.578 g), 1,1′-bis(3-aminopropyl)-4,4′-bipyridinium bis(hexafluorophosphate) (0.281 g, 0.5 mmol) and DMF (5.0 mL) were added into a 10 mL three-necked round flask with magnetic stirring under nitrogen atmosphere. Then 3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl isocyanate (0.297 g, 1.2 mmol) was added into above uniform dispersion. The CDs and viologen were decorated with siloxane through the reaction between amine and isocyanate groups at room temperature. Here, the resultant hydrolysable siloxane-decorated viologen was defined as Viologen-Si. Three hours later, the resultant mixture dispersion (Viologen-Si and CDs-Si) was drop-cast on the hydrophilic ITO substrate, followed by drying under vacuum at 40 °C for 2 h. Here, the hydrophilic ITO substrates were prepared according to the established method [34]. Subsequently, the mixture-decorated ITO substrate was employed as a working electrode to construct a conventional three-electrode cell system, with the assistance of Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and platinum wire counter electrode. The CV method was carried out in the potential range of −1.2 V–0 V with a scan rate of 100 mV/s in 0.1 M KCl electrolyte. No change was observed after 20 cycles, which indicates the accomplishment of the hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. The resultant composite was defined as VCG. Its thickness is about 330 nm measured by profilometer. The mass ratio of viologen:SiO2:CDs is calculated about 27:18:55. The detailed procedure was depicted in Fig. 2 .

The surface of CDs was modified with siloxane groups by chemical means. As shown in Fig. 3 a, CDs and CDs-Si DMF dispersion exhibit similar UV–vis absorption with the peaks centered around 340 nm and 370 nm. Moreover, the CDs DMF dispersion revealed an obvious fluorescent emission peak centered at 494 nm, while the CDs-Si DMF dispersion revealed a decrease in peak intensity at the same wavelength. The fluorescence images of CDs DMF dispersion and CDs-Si DMF dispersion were presented as the inset of Fig. 3a, which is in accord with the results from fluorescence spectroscopy. We use FTIR to confirm the modification of CDs with 3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl isocyanate (Fig. 3b). Obvious vibration peaks around 1236 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 demonstrated the formation of Si–C bonds. Also, the FTIR spectra showed C–H stretching vibration of alkyl groups around 2900 cm−1. From the TEM images (Fig. 3c), the size of CDs was found to be 9–13 nm. Modification of CDs with siloxane groups (CDs-Si) also shows similar size.

Hydrolysis reaction of siloxanes generally occurs in soluble or dispersive solvent under the catalysis of H+ or OH−. However, the CDs-Si and Viologen-Si monomers in this work were immobilized on the ITO substrate. The insertion of these catalytic ions through the solid mixture film was supposed to be difficult. In view of the electroactivity of viologen, cyclic voltammetry was carried out on the mixture film with the potentials of −1.2 V–0 V in 0.1 M KCl solution. Under electrokinetic potentials, hydrated ions are expedited to permeate into the solid film, which will trigger the hydrolysis reaction of siloxanes. Considering the tri-functionality of decorated siloxanes, the resultant polymer will be crosslinked. Therefore, such reaction was defined as an electrochemistry-assisted hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CV curves reveal a detailed electrokinetic crosslinking process. The CV curves exhibit two pairs of redox peaks at −0.97 V/-0.85 V and −0.53 V/-0.40 V, ascribed to the transitions of (V·+)/(V2+) and (V0)/(V·+) of viologen segments. The potential differences between oxidation peak and reduction peak are narrower than those of viologen hybrid glass composite film (−1.08 V/-0.80 V and −0.61 V/-0.35 V in Fig. S1), which indicated the increase of redox reversibility of VCG composite film, attributing to the coexistence of CDs. Moreover, the CV curves reveal a detailed electrokinetic crosslinking process. With the increase of CV cycles, the redox peak currents decrease gradually, which indicates that the insertion/extraction of hydrated ion through the electroactive film is impeded increasingly, ascribed to generation of compacted crosslinked structures under electrokinetic potential. After 20 CV cycles, no change is observed for the peak currents, indicating the complement of the hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. Compared to the common hydrolytic crosslinking reaction, this electrochemistry-assisted methodology shows rapidity and accessibility, which would have an important impact in synthesis of electroactive polysiloxane materials.

Meanwhile, EIS was also applied on the working electrode to provide insight into the process of hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. Nyquist plot of the VCG film was performed in the frequency range of 105 Hz–0.01 Hz with an AC amplitude of 5 mV, and the hold potential is fixed at −0.26 V. All the Nyquist plots of electroactive layer with different CV cycles were fitted by Randles type equivalent circuit model, which is usually used to simulate the experimental data at the initial state. Here, the film resistances (Rf) are determined by the intersecting point of the X-axis at the low-frequency end of the semicircle arc. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Nyquist plots disclose Rf value of 64000 Ω and 102000 Ω as the electroactive layer undergoes 1 and 5 CV cycles, respectively. Although the Nyquist plot can't give an exact Rf value from the incomplete arc after 10 CV cycles, the trend of arc implies the increase of Rf, due to its compacted and crosslinked structures. These results are in good accordance with CV experiments. In the hydrophilicity measurement, the contact angle of electroactive layer increases drastically from 17° to 52° after the hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. However, the resultant VCG film is still hydrophilic, due to the outstanding hydrophilic essence of viologen and CDs, which is beneficial to the electrolyte diffusion in the electrochemical measurements. Furthermore, the morphology of VCG composite is characterized by SEM (Fig. 4c). The inset shows SEM images of hydrolysable mixture (CDs-Si and Viologen-Si) coated on ITO substrate before the hydrolysis reaction. The drop-casting film appears to be flat with a few irregular particles. After the crosslinking reaction, the film becomes textured and layered, attributed to the molecule aggregation and release of small molecules during the crosslinking reaction.

As a typical electrochemically-modulated optical property, electrochromism is undoubtedly the most extensively studied, which is a phenomenon of reversible change in optical properties (reflectivity, transmissivity, absorptivity, etc.) of a material produced electrochemically, and the appearance presents reversible changes in color and transparency [35–42]. To keep up with commercial demand, most researchers focus on the exploitation of high-performance and/or multi-functional electrochromic materials [43–45]. Considering the intrinsic redox-switchable color of viologen, we examined the electrochromic behaviors of VCG composite by an optically transparent thin-layer electrode on electrochemical workstation coupled with UV–vis spectroscopy. The typical transmittance spectral changes at various applied potentials from 0 V to −1.20 V are shown in Fig. 5 a. The residence time in the spectroelectrochemical measurements is fixed at 300 s. As the applied potential increases gradually, two obvious absorptions appear at 400 nm and 550 nm, respectively. The absorption at 400 nm decreases gradually through the increase of potential from 0 V to −1.20 V. A great optical contrast value of 73% at 400 nm is revealed between 0 V and −1.2 V in Fig. 5a. However, it is noteworthy that the absorption at 550 nm firstly increases and then decreases with minimum transmittance at −0.8 V. Its optical contrast values are found to be 56% at 550 nm measured between its coloring (at −0.6 V and/or −0.8 V) and bleaching states (at 0 V). Moreover, the color of the VCG composite film changes drastically from transparent (at 0.0 V), to violet (at −0.6 V), to light pink (at −1.0 V) and finally to sandybrown (at −1.2 V) (inset of Fig. 5a), accompanied by the formation of the fully reduced state in the viologen segments (Fig. 5b).

Due to the unique redox states of viologen moiety (Fig. 5b), the VCG composite film exhibits an interesting multistage regulated electrochromic behavior. Therefore, the spectrochronoamperometry was performed on the VCG/ITO electrode under repeated potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V, respectively. The residence time here was fixed at 15 s. The transmittance contrast was monitored at 400 nm. The first 150 cycles under different potential steppings were collected and shown in Fig. 5c. The VCG composite film displays tiny decrease after 150 cycles, indicating good cycling stability in the electrochromic measurement. It is obvious that this remarkable electrochemical durability should be attributed to the robust crosslinked network structure of VOG composite. Moreover, the resultant VCG composite would be anchored to the ITO substrate through the hydrolysis reaction, because of the hydroxyl groups on the hydrophilic ITO substrate. These generated Si–O covalent bonds would also benefit to the improvement of cycling stability in electrochemical measurement. The transmittance contrast values are calculated about 33%, 27%, and 60% in the potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V, respectively, which are smaller than those from spectroelectrochemical measurements, ascribed to its short residence time. These results indicate that the VCG composite film discloses sequential multistage regulated feature at 400 nm in the potential range of 0~-1.2 V. The electrochromic coloration efficiency (η = ΔOD/Q) was measured by monitoring the amount of ejected charge (Q) as a function of the change in optical density (ΔOD) of the electroactive film. According to the transmittance contrast values and current consumptions, the coloration efficiency was calculated to be about 69, 152 and 87 cm2/C at 400 nm with the potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V, respectively. Moreover, the switching times in the electrochromic behavior have also been determined from the typical switching curves presented in Fig. 5d. The switching time is defined here as the time required for reaching 90% of the full change in a coloring/bleaching process. As shown in Fig. 5d, VCG composite film exhibits various switching times of 3.5/3.0 s, 3.6/7.5 s and 4.9/7.5 s for each coloring/bleaching process at the potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V, respectively. Compared with these reported electrochromic devices based on small viologen derivative molecules [46,47], the VCG composite demonstrates slower switching speed, attributed to its complicated crosslinked molecular architecture.

The transmittance contrast at 550 nm was also collected by spectrochronoamperometry. Fig. 5e shows the first 150 cycles with transmittance contrast value of 39%, 18%, and 20% under the potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V, respectively. However, it is obvious that transmittance change at the potential stepping of 0~-1.2 V undergoes a coloring/bleaching sequential process in once potential switching as shown in Fig. 5e. It is composed of a transmittance change in range of 0~-0.6 V and −0.6~-1.2 V, due to its minimum transmittance value at −0.6 V. These results are in agreement with the spectroelectrochemical measurements. The VCG composite film displays moderate coloration efficiency value of 72 and 53 cm2/C at 550 nm with the potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V and −0.6~-1.2 V, respectively. The switching times of VCG composite film under the potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V and −0.6~-1.2 V are found about 4.0/4.1 s and 4.5/4.0 s for each coloring/bleaching process. Similarly, these results demonstrate the multistage regulated feature of electrochromism of this VCG composite film at 550 nm in the potential range of 0~-1.2 V.

Electrofluorochromism, as a rising electrochemically-modulated optical property, deals with electrochemical switching of fluorescence features through the interplay between redox moiety and fluorescence emission moiety, which exhibits great potential for photoelectric conversion devices, biological information storage, chemical sensors and intelligent displays [48–53]. Considering the coexist of electroactive viologen moiety and fluorescent CDs, the electrofluorochromic behavior of the resultant VCG composite was investigated on electrochemical workstation coupled with fluorescence spectroscopy. Firstly, the spectroelectrochemical measurements were performed on VCG/ITO electrode using traditional three-electrode cell. Fig. 6 a shows the fluorescence change of VCG composite film under various potentials from 0 V to −1.2 V. The VCG/ITO electrode exhibits obvious fluorescence emission in the range of 500–750 nm with maximum emission at 600 nm. The fluorescence emission of VCG composite film exhibits a three-level decrease with the increase of applied potential from 0 V (and −0.2 V, −0.4 V) to −0.6 V (and −0.8 V), finally to −1.0 V (and −1.2 V), which indicates that the obvious fluorescence decrease would be closely related to the redox states of viologen moiety.

In addition, the fluorescence switching features were also studied using spectrochronoamperometry method. Fluorescence change of VCG/ITO electrode was monitored at 600 nm under different repeated potential steppings of 0~-0.6 V, −0.6~-1.2 V, and 0~-1.2 V with a residence time of 30 s. Fig. 6b shows the first 40 cycles under the three potential steppings without any attenuation, which indicates the good electrofluorochromic stability of VCG composite. Similarly, this good cycling stability should be ascribed to the robust crosslinked network structure of VCG composite and strong Si–O covalent bonds between VCG film and ITO substrate. These switching curves display a unique multistage regulated electrofluorochromic performance of VCG composite film in the potential range of 0~-1.2 V. Similarly, the switching time is defined as the time required for reaching 90% of the full change in an on/off process. As shown in Fig. 6c, the VCG composite film discloses quick switching times of 6.1 s at −0.6 V for its “off” state and 7.7 s at 0 V for its “on” state. The switching time is calculated about 8.2 s at −1.2 V for its “off” state and 15.9 s at −0.6 V for its “on” state. In the last potential stepping of 0~-1.2 V, switching time is calculated about 9.5 s at −1.2 V for its “off” state and 13.9 s at 0 V for its “on” state.

These results lead us to propose the principle for fluorescence switching with redox moieties. Firstly, the absorbance of viologen moieties in the range of 310–450 nm increased drastically with the elevation of applied potentials as shown in Fig. 5a. That means the intensity of the incident light of 410 nm decreased and carbon dots might not be able to fully absorb the incident light, resulting in a decrease of fluorescence emission. This phenomenon was determined as typical inner filter effect [54,55]. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs-Si/ITO and VCG/ITO were estimated to be approximately 7.82 ns and 3.97 ns, respectively (Fig. 6d). This obvious difference of lifetimes should be ascribed to the energy transfer between fluorescent carbon dots and redox viologen groups during the photoluminescence process. As the viologen segments were reduced to their reductive state (V·+ and V0), the resultant quinoid structure would be acted as excitation traps and quench fluorescence [56,57], which made the composite in the ‘off’ state. Obviously, the inner filter effect and energy transfer between carbon dots and viologen groups are co-responsible for this fluorescence switching upon the applied potentials.

As an emerging electrochemically-modulated optical property, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a luminescence produced through the interaction of excited species generated during the electrochemical reaction in solution [58,59], which proved to be very useful in analytical applications due to its fast response speeds, simple operation processes and good sensitivity [60,61]. In view of outstanding ECL performance of CDs in our previous research, we evaluate ECL property of VCG composite on electrochemical workstation coupled with the ultraweak detection system. The cathodic ECL measurement was performed on the VCG/ITO electrode with the assistance of potassium persulfate as oxidant. During ECL process, the fixed CDs in VCG composite transform into CDs·- radicals under highly energetic electrochemical reaction. Meanwhile, the reduction of S2O8 2− generates a strong oxidizing SO4·- radicals electronically. The combination of resultant CDs·- radicals and SO4·- radicals would form the emitters for ECL emission. As shown in Fig. 7 a, during the cyclic scanning between 0 and -2.5 V, the ECL peak from VCG/K2S2O8 system appears a maximum value at −2.40 V, which is a reduced trigger potential compared to pure CDs (2.50 V determined under same conditions). In Fig. 7b, the VCG composite exhibits an obvious lower ECL intensity than that from pure CDs (ECL intensity of 28000 determined under same conditions), ascribed to the decrease of surface area and low content of CDs in VCG composite. Moreover, this reduction of ECL intensity might be also associated with the redox viologen moiety in the VCG composite, which could quench those generated radicals partially. This disclosure represents the beginning of our research in this area. Future studies will aim to utilize this type of ECL materials bearing redox moiety in analytical applications.

As a summary, we have demonstrated a successful fabrication of optical composite containing electroactive viologen and fluorescent CDs through electrochemistry-assisted hydrolytic crosslinking reaction. The resultant composite film demonstrates elegant multiple electrochemically-modulated optical properties including electrochromism, electrofluorochromism, and electrochemiluminescence, ascribed to their inherent functionality and interplay between viologen and CDs. Given the easily available raw materials, rational design, and scalable preparation process, our work provides a promising strategy to acquire multiple electrochemically-modulated optical materials for enhancement of their applicable versatility.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We graciously acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China for funding (grant No. 21774046) and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Department, China (Grant No. 20190201075JC).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.108048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials and designs for wearable photodetectors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cai S, Xu X, Yang W, Chen J, Fang X. Materials and designs for wearable photodetectors. Adv Mater 2019;31:1808138.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: AIEgens-lightened functional polymers: synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou S, Wan H, Zhou F, Gu P, Xu F, Lu J. AIEgens-lightened functional polymers: synthesis, properties and applications. Chinese J Polym Sci 2019;37:302-326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color carbon dots with multiple red-emission tuning: on/off sensors, in vitro and in vivo multicolor bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Huo F, Liang W, Tang Y, Zhang W, Liu X, Pei D, Wang H, Jia W, Jia P, Yang F. Full-color carbon dots with multiple red-emission tuning: On/off sensors, in vitro and in vivo multicolor bioimaging. J Mater Sci 2019;54:6815-6825.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lbrahim L, Lim HN, Zawawi RM, Tajutin AA, Ng YH, Guo H, Huang NM. A review on visible-light induced photoelectrochemical sensors based on CdS nanoparticles. J Mater Chem B 2018;6:4551-4568.
